tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.details:
These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
 - VBLENDVPD: 8 occurrences<<list_path_1_complex_1>>
 - VPBLENDVB: 8 occurrences<<list_path_1_complex_2>>
 - VPEXTRQ: 16 occurrences<<list_path_1_complex_3>>
hint.title: Complex instructions

hint.workaround:
- Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
 - Try to remove indirect accesses. If applicable, precompute elements out of the innermost loop.
hint.details:
- Constant unknown stride: 11 occurrence(s)
 - Irregular (variable stride) or indirect: 4 occurrence(s)
Non-unit stride (uncontiguous) accesses are not efficiently using data caches
hint.title: Slow data structures access
hint.txt: Detected data structures (typically arrays) that cannot be efficiently read/written

hint.workaround:
Use vector aligned instructions:
 1) align your arrays on 32 bytes boundaries: replace { void *p = malloc (size); } with { void *p; posix_memalign (&p, 32, size); }.
 2) inform your compiler that your arrays are vector aligned: if array 'foo' is 32 bytes-aligned, define a pointer 'p_foo' as __builtin_assume_aligned (foo, 32) and use it instead of 'foo' in the loop.
<<image_vec_align>>
hint.title: Vector unaligned load/store instructions
hint.txt: Detected 20 suboptimal vector unaligned load/store instructions.

hint.title: Type of elements and instruction set
hint.txt: 72 AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (four at a time).

hint.title: Matching between your loop (in the source code) and the binary loop
hint.txt:
The binary loop is composed of 248 FP arithmetical operations:
 - 96: addition or subtraction (32 inside FMA instructions)
 - 128: multiply (32 inside FMA instructions)
 - 24: divide
The binary loop is loading 2144 bytes (268 double precision FP elements).
The binary loop is storing 784 bytes (98 double precision FP elements).

hint.title: Arithmetic intensity
hint.txt: Arithmetic intensity is 0.08 FP operations per loaded or stored byte.

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 110.00 cycles. At this rate:
 - 30% of peak load performance is reached (19.49 out of 64.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
 - 22% of peak store performance is reached (7.13 out of 32.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks
expert.txt:
Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 110.00 to 96.00 cycles (1.15x speedup).

header: 14% of peak computational performance is used (2.25 out of 16.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
- Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
gain.title: Code clean check
gain.txt:
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 110.00 to 96.00 cycles (1.15x speedup).

gain.workaround:
- Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
 - Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
C storage order is row-major: for(i) for(j) a[j][i] = b[j][i]; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) for(j) a[i][j] = b[i][j]; (fast, stride 1)<<image_row_maj>>
  * If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)
gain.details:
57% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
 - 29% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
 - 40% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
 - 82% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your loop is partially vectorized.
Only 61% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 110.00 to 80.09 cycles (1.37x speedup).

gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt: Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

potential.workaround:
Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
Estimated speedup by perfect pairing: 1.04x.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).
potential.title: FMA
potential.txt:
Detected 32 FMA (fused multiply-add) operations.
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.
